content.text: Portfolio Kill Criteria and Sunset Rules

A Claude portfolio without exit criteria is a portfolio that grows indefinitely — accumulating underperforming use cases, consuming CoE maintenance capacity, and obscuring signal about what actually works. Kill criteria and sunset rules are the mechanisms that keep the portfolio honest: they define the conditions under which a use case is terminated, and they remove the politics from what is otherwise a difficult and often-avoided conversation.

attrs.alt: Portfolio use case review decision flowchart

content.text: Why Kill Criteria Must Be Defined in Advance

Kill criteria are only effective when defined before a use case is deployed, not after. Post-hoc criteria are too easy to rationalize: the team that built the use case will find reasons why the underperformance is temporary, attributable to external factors, or about to improve. Pre-defined criteria remove this rationalization by establishing the standard when stakes are low and agreement is easy.
Defined at intake:

kill criteria should be part of the use case brief approved at the start of development. The BU owner who requests the use case agrees to the criteria at the same time they agree to provide resources for the pilot.
Reviewed by finance:

use case ROI projections that form the basis of the investment decision should include the criteria under which the investment would be terminated. This anchors the kill criteria in financial logic, not just operational metrics.
Communicated to users:

users of a production Claude use case should know that performance is monitored and the tool may be retired if it does not meet standards. Surprise retirements erode trust; expected retirements based on communicated criteria do not.
The organization that cannot kill underperforming AI use cases will eventually lose the credibility to invest in new ones. Every zombie use case drains capacity from the next high-value initiative.
The Three Categories of Kill Criteria
Category 1: Mandatory Kill — Safety and Compliance
Some conditions require immediate termination regardless of business value, user adoption, or sunk cost. These are non-negotiable:
Condition
Example
Required Action
Unresolved safety violation
The use case produces outputs that expose users to legal or physical risk that the team cannot remediate within 72 hours
Immediate shutdown. No remediation period.
Data breach or unauthorized data exposure
PII or confidential data is transmitted to an external system without authorization
Immediate shutdown. Security incident response. Regulatory notification if required.
Regulatory non-compliance confirmed
Legal or compliance confirms the use case violates a regulation (GDPR, HIPAA, SOX, etc.) and cannot be remediated while operational
Immediate shutdown. Remediation plan required before reinstatement.
Model manipulation confirmed
Evidence of successful prompt injection or adversarial manipulation affecting production outputs
Immediate shutdown. Security review before reinstatement.

There is no performance threshold that overrides a mandatory kill. A use case that processes 10,000 contracts per day and saves $2M/year is still immediately shut down if a data breach is confirmed. The cost of running a compromised system is always higher than the cost of the shutdown.
Category 2: Performance-Based Kill — Accuracy and Reliability
Use cases that fail to meet accuracy or reliability thresholds trigger a remediation window before termination:
Accuracy threshold:

each use case should have a defined minimum accuracy requirement established before deployment (see the tier governance framework). If measured accuracy falls below this threshold for 60 consecutive days despite optimization attempts, the use case enters a 30-day remediation window. If the threshold is not met after remediation, the use case is terminated.
Reliability threshold:
if the use case has an SLA (e.g., 99.5% uptime, < 5 second response time) and misses that SLA for 30 consecutive days due to systemic issues (not isolated incidents), trigger a remediation review.
Error rate escalation:

if the rate of AI errors requiring human correction (override rate) exceeds 40% for 60 days — meaning more than 4 in 10 AI outputs are wrong enough that users correct them — the use case is failing to provide net value. Enter remediation.

The 30-day remediation window requires a written remediation plan from the CoE technical lead within 5 business days of trigger. The plan must include: root cause analysis, specific changes to be made (prompt revision, model update, integration fix), and the expected accuracy improvement. If the plan cannot be written in 5 days, that is evidence the problem is not understood — escalate to the CoE director.
Category 3: Adoption-Based Kill — User and Business Engagement
A technically accurate use case that no one uses is not delivering value. Adoption thresholds:
Metric
Trigger Threshold
Timeframe
Action
Active users / target users
< 20% of target user base active monthly
90 days post-launch
Root cause analysis; fix or sunset
User override rate
> 40% of outputs manually corrected or ignored
60-day rolling average
Accuracy review; may trigger Category 2
Net Promoter Score (internal)
< 20 (or equivalent satisfaction below 3.5/5)
First 90-day survey
UX and prompt review; re-survey in 60 days
Business process integration
Fewer than 50% of target workflows using Claude output as primary input
6 months post-launch
Process re-engineering review or sunset
Low adoption is a symptom, not a diagnosis. Before triggering a sunset, the CoE must diagnose the root cause. Common causes and responses:
Awareness gap:
users do not know the tool exists or how to access it. Response: targeted enablement campaign, not sunset. Revisit adoption in 30 days.
UX friction:
the interface or workflow integration makes the tool harder to use than the manual process. Response: UX redesign. Revisit in 60 days.
Output quality mismatch:
outputs are technically accurate but formatted or styled in a way that users don't trust or find useful. Response: prompt refinement with user co-design. Revisit in 45 days.
Process fit failure:
the AI was designed for a process that has since changed, or was designed based on a misunderstood process. Response: process mapping workshop. May require significant re-scoping or sunset.
Champion departure:

the internal champion who drove adoption has left the organization. Response: identify a new champion. Without one, the sunset probability is high — escalate to BU leadership to confirm continued sponsorship.
The Sunset Process
A sunset is not a failure — it is the program learning what does not work and reallocating capacity to what does. A well-executed sunset:
Step 1: Sunset Decision and Communication (Week 1)
CoE director confirms the kill/sunset trigger has been met and is not in a remediation window
BU owner is notified in writing: trigger met, sunset decision made, timeline
Users are notified with at least 30 days advance notice (60 days for operationally critical use cases)
Notification includes: last day of service, alternative process for the work the AI was handling, and an invitation to provide feedback on why the use case did not meet expectations
Step 2: Documentation and Learning Capture (Weeks 1–3)
Before shutting down, capture the institutional learning. A sunset without a post-mortem wastes the information:

# Use Case Sunset Post-Mortem Template

Use Case: [name]
Deployment period: [start] to [end]
Sunset trigger: [Category 1 / 2 / 3 and specific condition]

What worked:
- [specific aspects of the use case that performed as expected]

What did not work:
- [specific failure modes: technical, process, adoption]

Root cause assessment:
- [1–3 sentence honest diagnosis of the primary cause of failure]

What we would do differently:
- [specific changes to requirements, design, or implementation that could have changed the outcome]

Could this use case succeed in a different form?
- [Yes / No / Maybe — with rationale]

Recommendations for the portfolio:
- [any changes to the intake process, tier classification, or governance based on this experience]

Captured by: [CoE lead name]
Date: [date]
Reviewed by: [CoE director, BU owner]
Step 3: Technical Shutdown (Week 3–4)
Disable access for all users on the announced last day
Archive all logs, prompt versions, and performance data for 24 months (regulatory minimum in most industries)
Remove API keys and integration credentials from the decommissioned workflow
Update the CoE service catalog to mark the use case as 'Retired' with the sunset date
Notify the IT team to remove any infrastructure provisioned for the use case (if applicable)
Step 4: Portfolio Reallocation (Week 4)
Sunset frees capacity. The CoE director reviews the capacity released and determines how to reallocate it:
Engineering capacity: are there higher-priority Tier 2 or 3 use cases in the backlog that can now move forward?
Budget: if the sunset use case had a dedicated CoE budget line, reallocate to the next approved use case or to CoE platform investment
Stakeholder goodwill: the BU whose use case was sunsetted should receive priority consideration for the next use case request if they engaged constructively in the post-mortem
Special Case: Externally Forced Sunset
Not all sunsets are driven by performance. Several external events can force retirement of a Claude use case regardless of its performance:
API deprecation:

Anthropic deprecates the model version the use case is pinned to. This is a planned sunset — Anthropic's model deprecation notices provide lead time (typically 6–12 months). Use the lead time to evaluate whether to migrate to the new model version, rebuild on a different architecture, or retire the use case.
Vendor exit:

for Buy or Partner sourcing model use cases, the vendor exits the market, is acquired, or discontinues the product. Maintain an exit plan for all vendor-dependent use cases — know what you would do if the vendor gave 90 days notice.
Regulatory change:

a new regulation prohibits or materially constrains the use case (e.g., a new AI transparency law requires disclosure mechanisms the use case does not have). Treat as a Category 1 trigger: shut down first, then evaluate what remediation would be required for reinstatement.
Organizational change:
a merger, acquisition, or restructuring eliminates the business unit or process the use case supported. Treat as a Category 3 trigger and follow the standard sunset process.
Kill Criteria by Portfolio Tier
Kill criteria thresholds differ by tier because the risk profile, investment level, and user expectations differ:
Criterion
Tier 1 (Quick Wins)
Tier 2 (Strategic Scale)
Tier 3 (High-Stakes)
Mandatory kill (safety/compliance)
Immediate
Immediate
Immediate
Accuracy threshold
70% (user self-corrects easily)
85% (errors reviewed by human)
95%+ (errors are costly)
Accuracy below threshold trigger
60 days below threshold
30 days below threshold
14 days below threshold
Remediation window
30 days
30 days
14 days (given higher risk)
Adoption threshold
20% of target monthly
40% of target monthly
60% of target monthly
Adoption trigger timeframe
90 days post-launch
60 days post-launch
30 days post-launch
User override rate trigger
> 40% for 60 days
> 30% for 30 days
> 20% for 14 days
Sunset notice period
14 days
30 days
60 days

Tier 3 use cases have tighter thresholds and shorter trigger windows because errors in high-stakes contexts propagate faster and more expensively than in Tier 1. A Tier 1 mistake in a meeting summary is self-correcting; a Tier 3 mistake in a financial decision might not surface for months.
Measuring Accuracy: Practical Methods for Enterprise Use Cases

Kill criteria that reference accuracy thresholds require a practical, repeatable method to measure accuracy. This is harder than it sounds for most Claude use cases, because unlike a traditional software function, AI output quality is not binary. A contract summary that omits one minor clause is different from one that misidentifies the governing law.
Sampling-Based Accuracy Measurement
For most production use cases, measuring every output is not feasible. Use stratified random sampling:
Sample size:

review a minimum of 50 outputs per measurement period for Tier 1 use cases, 100 for Tier 2, and 200 for Tier 3. Below 50 samples, accuracy estimates are too noisy to trust — a single bad batch will trigger false alarms.
Stratified by input type:

if the use case processes different types of inputs (e.g., different contract types, different document lengths, different languages), ensure your sample includes representation from each type. A sample that over-represents easy cases will give an inflated accuracy estimate.
Blind review:

the person reviewing accuracy should not know which outputs were flagged as low-confidence by the model. If reviewers know which outputs the model was unsure about, they will over-scrutinize those and under-scrutinize confident outputs, distorting the measurement.
Structured rubric:

reviewers score each output against a rubric with 3–5 dimensions (e.g., factual accuracy, completeness, format compliance, tone appropriateness) rather than a single overall quality score. This identifies which dimension is degrading, which is essential for writing a useful remediation plan.
Ground Truth for Accuracy Measurement
Accuracy measurement requires a ground truth — the correct answer to compare against. For different use case types:
Use Case Type
Ground Truth Source
Reviewer
Document classification
Expert human classification of the same documents
Domain expert (legal, compliance, etc.)
Summarization
Human-written summary of the same source material
The person who would normally write the summary
Q&A over knowledge base
Expert-verified answer key for a representative question set
SME who owns the knowledge domain
Code generation
Human-written implementation that passes the same test suite
Senior engineer in the relevant language
Data extraction
Manually extracted fields from the same documents
Process owner or trained data entry staff
Decision support (recommendation)
Historical expert decisions on the same cases (where available)
Domain expert reviewing the reasoning, not just the conclusion

For use cases where no ground truth exists (the AI is doing something that no one did before), substitute user override rate: what percentage of AI outputs does the user change before acting on them? An override rate above 30% is a strong signal that users do not trust the output, even if they cannot articulate a specific error.
The Remediation Window: What Good Remediation Looks Like

The 30-day remediation window is not a grace period — it is a structured recovery process. A use case that spends 30 days with no substantive changes and then is measured again has not been remediated; it has been deferred. The CoE must distinguish between active remediation and passive waiting.
Elements of a Valid Remediation Plan
Within 5 business days of triggering the remediation window, the CoE technical lead must submit a written remediation plan covering:
Root cause (specific):

not 'prompt quality issues' but 'the classification prompt does not handle multi-party agreements with addenda — these account for 38% of the error cases.' The root cause must be specific enough that a different engineer could read it and know what to fix.
Changes to be made:

list the specific changes: which prompts are being revised, what new examples are being added to few-shot demonstrations, what integration changes are being made, or what process changes are being implemented to reduce error-prone input types.
Expected accuracy improvement:

a quantified estimate of the accuracy improvement expected from each change, with reasoning. If the root cause accounts for 38% of errors and you are fixing it, expect to recover at most 38 percentage points of accuracy — likely less.
Testing plan:
how will the changes be tested before being deployed to production? Include the test set size, the review process, and the minimum threshold to proceed to production.
Measurement date:
the specific date at which accuracy will be re-measured after changes are deployed. This is the milestone at which the use case either passes or is terminated.
A remediation plan without a measurement date is a delay, not a plan. Every remediation window must end with a binary decision: the use case passes the re-measurement threshold, or it is terminated.
Common Remediation Patterns and Their Effectiveness
Not all remediation approaches are equally effective. Understanding which changes actually move the needle helps CoE teams invest their remediation window wisely:
Remediation Approach
Typical Accuracy Gain
Best For
Prompt revision with error analysis
5–20 percentage points
Most use cases — always try this first
Adding few-shot examples (3–10 examples)
5–15 percentage points
Classification tasks; complex formatting requirements
Chain-of-thought instruction refinement
10–25 percentage points
Reasoning-heavy tasks; multi-step analysis
Input preprocessing (cleaning, normalization)
5–15 percentage points
Use cases with noisy or inconsistent input data
Output post-processing (validation, formatting)
2–10 percentage points
Format compliance failures; structured output issues
Scope reduction (remove problematic input types)
Up to 30 percentage points
When a subset of inputs account for most errors — narrow the use case scope
Model version upgrade
Varies (can also regress)
After all prompt-level changes have been tried

Scope reduction deserves special attention: if analysis shows that 70% of accuracy failures come from a specific input type (e.g., non-English documents, scanned PDFs with poor OCR quality, inputs longer than 50,000 tokens), removing that input type from the use case scope can dramatically improve measured accuracy. This is not cheating — it is honest scoping. Document the exclusion clearly and ensure users are redirected to an appropriate process for the excluded inputs.
Portfolio Health Dashboard
Kill criteria work best when they are part of a portfolio health dashboard that makes the status of every use case visible to CoE leadership and BU stakeholders. A portfolio health dashboard:
Shows every active use case with current status (green / yellow / red) based on the kill criteria dimensions
Displays the trend line for each key metric (accuracy, adoption, override rate) over the last 90 days — a declining trend at 80% is more concerning than a stable 75%
Highlights any use cases currently in a remediation window, with the remediation deadline and plan summary
Shows the quarter-by-quarter sunset history — how many use cases were terminated, and why
Is updated at least monthly and reviewed in the CoE's monthly steering committee

# Portfolio Health Dashboard Schema (example)

use_case: Contract Review Assistant
tier: 2
status: YELLOW  # green / yellow / red
last_updated: 2025-Q2

metrics:
  accuracy:
    current: 81.2%
    threshold: 85%
    trend: declining (-3.4pp over 90 days)
    status: YELLOW  # within threshold but declining
  adoption:
    current_monthly_active: 34 of 60 target users (57%)
    threshold: 40% (24 users)
    trend: stable
    status: GREEN
  override_rate:
    current: 22%
    threshold: 30%
    trend: increasing (+5pp over 60 days)
    status: YELLOW

remediation:
  active: false
  last_review: 2025-05-15
  notes: "Accuracy decline driven by new contract template introduced in April.
          Prompt update in progress. Expected resolution by end of Q2."

next_review_date: 2025-07-01
Communicating Portfolio Decisions to Stakeholders
Kill decisions involve people — the users who relied on the tool, the BU leaders who sponsored it, and the CoE team that built it. Communication matters:
Communicating a Kill to Users
Users receive a sunset notice with:
Clear statement of the last day the tool will be available
Brief explanation of why (accuracy below standard, low adoption, compliance issue) — written in plain language, not CoE jargon
Alternative: what they should do instead after the tool is gone
Feedback opportunity: a short survey or contact for feedback on why the tool did or did not work for them
Data: if users have personal outputs or history they want to retain, how to export it before shutdown
Communicating a Kill to BU Leadership
BU leaders receive a separate communication that:
Acknowledges the investment made and the effort of the team
States the trigger criterion that was met, with the measured data
Summarizes what was learned from the post-mortem
Proposes a path forward: either a redesigned version that addresses the failure mode, or a recommendation to redirect the investment to a different use case
Expresses continued partnership — a sunset is the end of one use case, not the end of the CoE's engagement with the BU

The BU sponsor of a sunsetted use case should leave the process feeling that the CoE handled it professionally and that the program is rigorous about quality — not that the CoE gave up on them. How a sunset is communicated determines whether that BU comes back with the next use case.
Preventing Unnecessary Sunsets: Early Warning System

The best sunset is the one that does not happen because a problem was caught and corrected early. An early warning system gives the CoE 30–60 days of lead time to address a declining use case before it crosses a kill threshold:
Trend alerts (not threshold alerts):

configure monitoring to alert the CoE when a metric is declining consistently for 21 days — even if it has not yet breached the threshold. A 3-week downward trend in accuracy is an early warning; waiting for the 60-day threshold breach leaves half the available recovery time unused.
User feedback integration:

capture user feedback in-product (thumbs up/down, optional comment) and review it weekly. Users often report problems in feedback before they appear in accuracy metrics — they know the output is wrong before the measurement period catches it.
Input distribution monitoring:

monitor the distribution of input types over time. A shift in input distribution (e.g., more non-English documents after an acquisition, longer documents after a policy change) predicts an accuracy change before it is measured. Prompt updates can be pre-emptive rather than reactive.
Quarterly proactive reviews:

beyond metric monitoring, schedule a quarterly 30-minute review for every Tier 2 and Tier 3 use case, even when metrics are green. These reviews surface soft signals — anecdotal reports from the champion, changes in the underlying business process, upcoming regulatory changes — that quantitative monitoring misses.
Summary

Portfolio kill criteria and sunset rules are the mechanisms that keep a Claude program honest, efficient, and credible. Define three categories of criteria: mandatory kills (safety and compliance — immediate, no exceptions), performance-based kills (accuracy and reliability — trigger a remediation window), and adoption-based kills (usage and engagement — require root cause diagnosis before acting). Execute sunsets with formal communication, a post-mortem that captures institutional learning, clean technical shutdown, and explicit capacity reallocation. Tier the kill thresholds to match the risk and investment level of each use case. A program that can kill underperforming use cases is a program that can continue to invest in new ones.
